title: Dog Black Crusty Skin on Elbows: Causes and Treatment
meta_desc: Wondering why your dog has black crusty skin on their elbows? Learn about pressure calluses, urgency levels, and how to help your pet feel better.
content_html:
TL;DR: Dark, crusty skin on a dog's elbows is typically a benign pressure callus caused by hard surfaces and rarely requires emergency care unless signs of infection are present.

What causes the black, crusty skin on my dog's elbows?

  • In the vast majority of cases, thick, dark, or crusty skin on the elbows is a pressure callus caused by resting on hard surfaces like tile, hardwood, or concrete.
  • To protect the underlying bone, the skin produces extra keratin, causing it to become thick, hairless, and dark in color (hyperpigmentation).

Is the black or crusty skin on my dog's elbows a veterinary emergency?

The urgency level for this condition is Low, but you should seek veterinary attention if you notice signs of secondary infection, such as:

  • Redness or warmth around the area
  • Bleeding
  • The presence of pus (a condition called callus pyoderma)

How does providing a photo of my dog's skin help with veterinary triage?

  • A clear, well-lit photo allows a professional to distinguish between a standard protective callus and other skin issues like mange, fungal infections, or tumors.
  • Seeing the texture and borders of the skin helps determine if a change in bedding is sufficient or if a physical exam is necessary to rule out deeper issues.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Crusty skin on the elbows of dogs can be caused by several conditions. Sarcoptic mange (scabies) can present with papulocrustous eruptions, excoriation, erythema, and alopecia initially affecting the ventral abdomen, chest, ears, elbows, and hocks. Untreated, this can generalize, leading to seborrhea, severe thickening of the skin with fold formation and crust buildup. Familial zinc-responsive dermatopathy, seen in breeds like Alaskan Malamutes and Huskies, can cause crusting and hyperkeratosis of the extremities. Secondary Malassezia infections are common in these cases. Secondary acanthosis nigricans can also manifest as hyperpigmentation and crusting in the axillary and inguinal regions secondary to inflammation from conformational abnormalities, obesity, endocrinopathies, pruritus associated with atopic dermatitis, food allergy, contact dermatitis, primary disorders of keratinization, and skin infections.

Chapter: Dermatology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 970)

faqs.question: Can I use human moisturizer on my dog's elbows?
faqs.answer: It is best to avoid human lotions as they may contain ingredients that are toxic if licked. Use a pet-specific 'elbow butter' or coconut oil if recommended by your vet.

faqs.question: How can I prevent my dog's calluses from getting worse?
faqs.answer: The most effective way is to provide soft, padded bedding and encourage your dog to avoid laying on hard floors. Orthopedic beds are particularly helpful for larger breeds.

faqs.question: When should I be worried about an elbow callus?
faqs.answer: Schedule a vet visit if the callus begins to crack, bleed, drain fluid, or if your dog seems to be in pain when the area is touched.
